title: THE INTRINSIC DIMENSION OF IMAGES AND ITS IMPACT ON LEARNING [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## THE INTRINSIC DIMENSION OF IMAGES AND ITS IMPACT ON LEARNING

**Phillip Pope**[1] **, Chen Zhu**[1] **, Ahmed Abdelkader**[2] **, Micah Goldblum**[1] **, Tom Goldstein**[1] 1Department of Computer Science, University of Maryland, College Park 2Oden Institute for Computational Engineering and Sciences, University of Texas at Austin _{_ pepope,chenzhu _}_ @umd.edu, akader@utexas.edu, _{_ goldblum,tomg _}_ @umd.edu

## ABSTRACT

It is widely believed that natural image data exhibits low-dimensional structure despite the high dimensionality of conventional pixel representations. This idea underlies a common intuition for the remarkable success of deep learning in computer vision. In this work, we apply dimension estimation tools to popular datasets and investigate the role of low-dimensional structure in deep learning. We find that common natural image datasets indeed have very low intrinsic dimension relative to the high number of pixels in the images. Additionally, we find that low dimensional datasets are easier for neural networks to learn, and models solving these tasks generalize better from training to test data. Along the way, we develop a technique for validating our dimension estimation tools on synthetic data generated by GANs allowing us to actively manipulate the intrinsic dimension by controlling the image generation process. Code for our experiments may be found here.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The idea that real-world data distributions can be described by very few variables underpins machine learning research from manifold learning to dimension reduction (Besold & Spokoiny, 2019; Fodor, 2002). The number of variables needed to describe a data distribution is known as its _intrinsic dimension_ (ID). In applications, such as crystallography, computer graphics, and ecology, practitioners depend on data having low intrinsic dimension (Valle & Oganov, 2010; Desbrun et al., 2002; Laughlin, 2014). The utility of representations which are low-dimensional has motivated a variety of deep learning techniques including autoencoders and regularization methods (Hinton & Salakhutdinov, 2006; Vincent et al., 2010; Gonzalez & Balajewicz, 2018; Zhu et al., 2018).

It is also known that dimensionality plays a strong role in learning function approximations and non-linear class boundaries. The exponential cost of learning in high dimensions is easily captured by the trivial case of sampling a function on a cube; in _d_ dimensions, sampling only the cube vertices would require 2 _[d]_ measurements. Similar behaviors emerge in learning theory. It is known that learning a manifold requires a number of samples that grows exponentially with the manifold’s intrinsic dimension (Narayanan & Mitter, 2010). Similarly, the number of samples needed to learn a well-conditioned decision boundary between two classes is an exponential function of the intrinsic dimension of the manifold on which the classes lie (Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009). Furthermore, these learning bounds have no dependence on the ambient dimension in which manifold-structured datasets live.

In light of the exponentially large sample complexity of learning high-dimensional functions, the ability of neural networks to learn from image data is remarkable. Networks learn complex decision boundaries from small amounts of image data (often just a few hundred or thousand samples per class). At the same time, generative adversarial networks (GANs) are able to learn image “manifolds” from merely a few thousand samples. The seemingly low number of samples needed to learn these manifolds strongly suggests that image datasets have extremely low-dimensional structure.

Despite the established role of low dimensional data in deep learning, little is known about the intrinsic dimension of popular datasets and the impact of dimensionality on the performance of neural networks. Computational methods for estimating intrinsic dimension enable these measurements.

Figure 1: Estimates of the intrinsic dimension of commonly used datasets obtained using the MLE method with _k_ = 3 _,_ 5 _,_ 10 _,_ 20 nearest neighbors (left to right). The trends are consistent using different _k_ ’s.
We adopt tools from the dimension estimation literature to shed light on dimensionality in settings of interest to the deep learning community. Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

- We verify the reliability of intrinsic dimension estimation on high-dimensional data using generative adversarial networks (GANs), a setting in which we can a priori upper-bound the intrinsic dimension of generated data by the dimension of the latent noise vector.

- We measure the dimensionality of popular datasets such as MNIST, CIFAR-10, and ImageNet. In our experiments, we find that natural image datasets whose images contain thousands of pixels can, in fact, be described by orders of magnitude fewer variables. For example, we estimate that ImageNet, despite containing 224 _×_ 224 _×_ 3 = 150528 pixels per image, only has intrinsic dimension between 26 and 43; see Figure 1.

- We train classifiers on data, synthetic and real, of various intrinsic dimension and find that this variable correlates closely with the number of samples needed for learning. On the other hand, we find that extrinsic dimension, the dimension of the ambient space in which data is embedded, has little impact on generalization.

Together, these results put experimental weight behind the hypothesis that the unintuitively low dimensionality of natural images is being exploited by deep networks, and suggest that a characterization of this structure is an essential building block for a successful theory of deep learning.

## 2 RELATED WORK

While the hypothesis that natural images lie on or near a low-dimensional manifold is controversial, Goodfellow et al. (2016) argue that the low-dimensional manifold assumption is at least approximately correct for images, supported by two observations. First, natural images are locally connected, with each image surrounded by other highly similar images reachable through image transformations (e.g., contrast, brightness). Second, natural images seem to lie on a low-dimensional structure, as the probability distribution of images is highly concentrated; uniformly sampled pixels can hardly assemble a meaningful image. It is widely believed that the combination of natural scenes and sensor properties yields very sparse and concentrated image distributions, as has been supported by several empirical studies on image patches (Lee et al., 2003; Donoho & Grimes, 2005; Carlsson et al., 2008). This observation motivated work on efficient coding (Olshausen & Field, 1996) and served as a prior in computer vision (Peyr´e, 2009). Further, rigorous experiments have been conducted clearly supporting the low-dimensional manifold hypothesis for many image datasets (Ruderman, 1994; Sch¨olkopf et al., 1998; Roweis & Saul, 2000; Tenenbaum et al., 2000; Brand, 2003); see also (Fefferman et al., 2016) for principled algorithms on verifying the manifold hypothesis.

The generalization literature seeks to understand why some models generalize better from training data to test data than others. One line of work suggests that the loss landscape geometry explains why neural networks generalize well (Huang et al., 2019). Other generalization work predicts that data with low dimension, along with other properties which do not include extrinsic dimension,

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

characterize the generalization difficulty of classification problems (Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009). In the context of deep learning, Gong et al. (2019) found that neural network features are lowdimensional. Ansuini et al. (2019) further found that the intrinsic dimension of features decreases in late layers of neural networks and observed interesting trends in the dimension of features in early layers. In contrast to Gong et al. (2019) and Ansuini et al. (2019), who find that the intrinsic dimension of internal representations is inversely correlated with high performance, we study the dimensionality of _data_ and its impact on performance, and we make a similar finding. Zhu et al. (2018) proposed a regularizer derived from the intrinsic dimension of images augmented with their corresponding feature vectors. Another line of work in deep learning has found that neural networks rely heavily on textures which are low-dimensional (Geirhos et al., 2018; Brendel & Bethge, 2019). Similarly, some have suggested that natural images can be represented as mixtures of textures which lie on a low-dimensional manifold (Vacher & Coen-Cagli, 2019; Vacher et al., 2020).

## 3 INTRINSIC DIMENSION ESTIMATION

Given a set of sample points _P ⊂_ R _[N]_ , it is common to assume that _P_ lies on or near a lowdimensional manifold _M ⊆_ R _[N]_ of intrinsic dimension dim( _M_ ) = _d ≪ N_ . As a measure of the degrees of freedom in a dataset, as well as the information content, there is great interest in estimating the intrinsic dimension _d_ . In the remainder of this section, we briefly describe the dimension estimation method we use in this paper; for further information, see (Kim et al., 2019) and references therein.

One of the main approaches to intrinsic dimension estimation is to examine a neighborhood around each point in the dataset, and compute the Euclidean distance to the _k[th]_ nearest neighbor. Assuming that density is constant within small neighborhoods, the _Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)_ of Levina & Bickel (2005) uses a Poisson process to model the number of points found by random sampling within a given radius around each sample point. By relating the rate of this process to the surface area of the sphere, the likelihood equations yield an estimate of the ID at a given point _x_ as:

(2005)where _T_ propose _j_ ( _x_ ) is theto averageEuclideanthe( _ℓ_ local2) distanceestimatesfromat _x_ eachto itspoint _j[th]_ nearestto obtainneighbor.a globalLevinaestimate& _m_ Bickel¯ _k_ = _n_ 1 � _ni_ =1 _[m]_[ˆ] _[k]_[(] _[x][i]_[)][.][MacKay][&][Ghahramani][(2005)][suggestion][a][correction][based][on][averaging][of] inverses

where _n_ is the number of samples. We use Equation (2) as our MLE estimator throughout this paper.

Since the geometry of natural images is complex and unknown, we face two challenges when verifying the accuracy of MLE on natural image datasets. First, we need to choose a proper value of _k_ . As shown by MacKay & Ghahramani (2005), the positive bias of the corrected estimator Equation (2) increases as _k_ increases, but the variance decreases. In order to navigate this bias-variance tradeoff, we try various values of _k_ in Section 4. Second, in addition to the aforementioned local uniformity assumption, MLE assumes that data arises as a sequence of i.i.d. random variables which can be written as a continuous and sufficiently smooth function of a random variable with smooth density, which may or may not be true for natural image datasets. While the truth of these assumptions is unknown on natural images, we verify the accuracy of our MLE estimates in a controlled setting in the following section.

We briefly discuss other notable techniques for dimensionality estimation. GeoMLE (Gomtsyan et al., 2019) attempts to account for non-uniformity of density and nonlinearity of manifold using a polynomial regression of standard MLE based on distances to nearest neighbors in different sized neighborhoods. However, GeoMLE chooses to approximate averages of _m_ ˆ _k_ ( _xi_ ), instead of

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

averaging its reciprocal like Equation (2), resulting in a potentially wrong maximum likelihood estimator. As a result, we find its estimation deviates significantly from expected dimensionalities. TwoNN (Facco et al., 2017) is based on the ratio of the distances to the first and second nearest neighbors. Finally, the approach of (Granata & Carnevale, 2016) considers the distribution of geodesic distances over the data manifold, approximated by distances through kNN graphs, compared to the distribution of distances over hyperspheres of varying dimension. Unlike MLE, our preliminary experiments suggest that these techniques do not provide reasonable estimates for some natural and synthetic images which are key to this work; see Appendix A.5 for further discussion.

## 4 VALIDATING DIMENSION ESTIMATION WITH SYNTHETIC DATA

Dimensionality estimates are often applied on “simple” manifolds or toy datasets where the dimensionality is known, and so the accuracy of the methods can be validated. Image manifolds, by contrast, are highly complex, may contain many symmetries and modes, and are of unknown dimension. In principle, there is no reason why MLE-based dimensionality estimates cannot be applied to image datasets. However, because we lack knowledge of the exact dimensionality of image datasets, we cannot directly verify that MLE-based dimensionality estimates scale up to the complexity of image structures.

There is an inherent uncertainty in estimating the ID of a given dataset. First, we cannot be sure if the dataset actually resembles a sampling of points on or near a manifold. Second, there are typically no guarantees that the sampling satisfies the conditions assumed by the ID estimators we are using.

Towards a principled application of ID estimates in contexts of practical relevance to deep learning, we begin by validating that MLE methods can generate accurate dimensionality estimates for complex image data. We do this by generating synthetic image datasets using generative models for which the intrinsic dimensionality can be upper-bounded a priori. We believe such validations are essential to put recent findings in perspective (Gong et al., 2019; Ansuini et al., 2019).

**GAN Images** We use the BigGAN variant with 128 latent entries and outputs of size 128 _×_ 128 _×_ 3 trained on the ImageNet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). Using this GAN, we generate datasets with a varying number of images, where we fix most entries of the latent vectors to zero leaving only _d_[¯] free entries to be chosen at random. As we increase the number of free entries, we expect the intrinsic dimension to increase with _d_[¯] as an upper bound; see Section A.1 for further discussion.

In particular, we create several synthetic datasets of varying intrinsic dimensionality using the ImageNet class, basenji, and check if the estimates match our expectation. As seen in Figure 2, we observe increasing diversity with increasing intrinsic dimension. In Figure 3, we show convergence of the MLE estimate on basenji data with dimension bounded above by _d_[¯] = 10. We observe that the estimates can be sensitive to the choice of _k_ as discussed in prior work; see Appendix A.2 for additional GAN classes.

**Scaling to large datasets.** We develop a practical approach for estimating the ID of large datasets such as ImageNet. In this approach, we randomly select a fraction _α_ of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset. Note that, when anchors are chosen randomly, this acceleration has no impact on the expected value of the result. See Appendix A.3 for an evaluation of this approach.

## 5 THE INTRINSIC DIMENSION OF POPULAR DATASETS

In this section, we measure the intrinsic dimensions of a number of popular datasets including MNIST (Deng, 2012), SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009), ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009), MS-COCO (Lin et al., 2014), and CelebA (Liu et al., 2015). Using three different parameter settings for the MLE ID estimator, we find that the ID is indeed much smaller than the number of pixels; see Table 5. Notice that the rank order of datasets by dimension does not depend on the choice of _k_ . A comparison of state-of-the-art (SOTA) classification accuracy on each respective dataset[1] with the dimension estimates suggests a negative correlation

between the intrinsic dimension and test accuracy. In the next section, we take a closer look at this phenomenon through a series of dedicated experiments.

## 6 INTRINSIC DIMENSION AND GENERALIZATION

Learning theory work has established that learning a manifold requires a number of samples that grows exponentially with the manifold’s intrinsic dimension (Narayanan & Mitter, 2010), but the required number of samples is independent of the extrinsic dimension. Specifically, the number of
Table 1: The MLE estimates for practical image datasets, and the state-of-the-art test-set image classification accuracy (for classification problems only) for these datasets.
samples needed to learn a well-conditioned decision boundary between two classes is exponential in the intrinsic dimension of the manifold on which the classes lie (Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009).

We leverage dimension estimation tools to empirically verify these theoretical findings using a family of binary classification problems defined over both synthetic and real datasets of varying intrinsic dimension. In these experiments, we observe a connection between the intrinsic dimension of data and generalization. Specifically, we find that classification problems on data of lower intrinsic dimensionality are easier to solve.

- 6.1 SYNTHETIC GAN DATA: SAMPLE COMPLEXITY DEPENDS ON INTRINSIC (NOT EXTRINSIC) DIMENSIONALITY

The synthetic GAN data generation technique described in Section 4 provides a unique opportunity to test the relationship between generalization and the intrinsic/extrinsic dimensionality of images. By creating datasets with controlled intrinsic dimensionality, we may compare their _sample complexity_ , that is the number of samples required to obtain a given level of test error. Specifically we test the following two hypotheses (1) data of lower intrinsic dimensionality has lower sample complexity than that of higher intrinsic dimensionality and (2) extrinsic dimensionality is irrelevant for sample complexity.

To investigate hypothesis (1), we create four synthetic datasets of varying intrinsic dimensionality: 16 _,_ 32 _,_ 64 _,_ 128, _fixed_ extrinsic dimensionality: 3 _×_ 32 _×_ 32, and two classes: basenji and beagle. For each dataset we fix a test set of size _N_ = 1700. For all experiments, we use the ResNet-18 (width = 64) architecture (He et al., 2016). We then train models until they fit their entire training set with increasing amounts of training samples and measure the test error. We show these results in Figure 4. Observing the varying rates of growth, we see that data of higher intrinsic dimension requires more samples to achieve a given test error.

For hypothesis (2), we carry out the same experiment with the roles of intrinsic and extrinsic dimension switched. We create four synthetic datasets of varying _extrinsic_ dimensionality by resizing the images with nearest-neighbor interpolation. Specifically we create 6 datasets of square, 3-channel images of sizes 16 _,_ 32 _,_ 64 _,_ 128 _,_ 256, _fixed_ intrinsic dimensionality of size 128, and all other experimental details the same. We show these results in Figure 5. Observing the lack of variable growth rates, we see that extrinsic dimension has little to no effect on sample complexity.
To the best of our knowledge, this is the first experimental demonstration that _intrinsic but not extrinsic dimensionality matters for the generalization of deep networks_ .

## 6.2 REAL DATA: INTRINSIC DIMENSIONALITY MATTERS FOR GENERALIZATION

Next, we examine the sample complexity of binary classification tasks from four common image datasets: MNIST, SVHN, CIFAR-10, and ImageNet. This case differs from the synthetic case in that we have no control over each dataset’s intrinsic dimension. Instead, we estimate it via the MLE method discussed in Section 3. To account for variable difficulty of classes, we randomly sample 5 class pairs from each dataset and run the previously described sample complexity experiment. Note that these subsets differ from those used in Table 5, where the estimates are taken from the entire dataset and across all classes.
On these sampled subsets, we find the MLE estimates as shown in Table 2. Note that these estimates are consistent with expectation, e.g. MNIST is qualitatively simpler then SVHN or CIFAR-10.

Figure 5: Sample complexity of synthetic datasets of varying extrinsic dimensionality.

We conduct the same sample complexity experiment as the previous section on the datasets. Because these datasets are ordinarily of varying extrinsic dimensionality, we resize all to size 32 _×_ 32 _×_ 3 (before applying MLE). We report results in Figure 6, where we overall observe trends ordered by intrinsic dimensionality estimate. These results are consistent with expectation of the relative hardness of each dataset. However, there are some notable differences from the synthetic case. Several unexpected cross-over points exist in the low-sample regime, and the gap between SVHN and CIFAR-10 is smaller than one may expect based on their estimated intrinsic dimension.
From these observations we conclude that intrinsic dimensionality is indeed relevant to generalization on real data, but it is not the only feature of data that influences sample complexity.

Table 2: Mean and standard error of estimated intrinsic dimensions for practical datasets under the same resolution 32 _×_ 32 _×_ 3 using different _k_ ’s. These results are consistent with the test accuracies on these datasets under the same resolution.

## 6.3 REAL DATA: ADDING NOISE CHANGES DIMENSIONALITY TO AFFECT GENERALIZATION

In this section, we examine an alternative technique for changing the intrinsic dimension of a _real_ dataset: adding noise to images. Here we leverage the fact that uniformly sampled noise in [0 _,_ 1 ~~]~~ _[d]_ has dimension _d_ . We thus add independent noise, drawn uniformly from a fixed randomly oriented _d_ -dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, to each sample in a dataset. This procedure ensures that the dataset has dimension at least _d_ . Since the natural data we use has low dimension, and the hypercubes have high dimension, this procedure specifically increases dimensionality. We note that estimation error may occur when there is an insufficient number of samples to achieve

a proper estimate. Since the variation in images in a dataset may still be dominated by non-noise directions, we expect to underestimate the new increased dimensions of these noised datasets.

Starting with CIFAR-10 data, we add noise of varying dimensions, where we replace pixels at random in the image. We only add noise to an image once to keep the augmented dataset the same size as the original. We use the following noise dimensionalities: 256 _,_ 512 _,_ 1024 _,_ 2048 _,_ 2560. The estimated dimensions of the noised datasets are listed in Table 3. We see that intrinsic dimension increases with increasing noise dimensionality, but dimensionality does not saturate to the maximum true dimension, likely due to a poverty of samples.

On these noisy CIFAR-10 datasets, we again carry out the sample complexity experiment of the previous sections. We show results in Figure 7. We observe sample complexity largely in the same order as intrinsic dimension.

## 6.4 MANIPULATING THE INTRINSIC DIMENSIONALITY OF FONTS

In this section, we describe a final technique for studying the effect of intrinsic dimensionality on sample complexity on the recently proposed FONTS dataset (Stutz et al., 2019). Beginning with a collection of characters and font types, termed a _prototype_ set by the authors, FONTS datasets are constructed using a fixed set of data augmentations: scaling, translation, rotation, and sheering. In

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

principle, these augmentations each increase the intrinsic dimension of the prototype set allowing us to synthetically alter the intrinsic dimension by varying the number of augmentations used.

We construct 5 FONTS datasets in this way, FONTS- _{_ 0 _,_ 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 4 _}_ , where the suffix denotes the number of transformations used in the data generation process. The MLE estimates on each of the datasets are given in Table 4.

Consistent with expectation, we observe that MLE methods consistently resolve the increased dimesionality of transformed datasets. Carrying out the sample complexity experiment of the previous section, we report results in Figure 8. We observe _again_ that, on the whole, sample complexity is ordered by intrinsic dimension.

Table 4: Mean and standard errror of MLE estimates on the FONTS dataset under different number of transforms using different _k_ ’s. Again, the ranks of the estimated IDs are consistent, and the estimated IDs increase with the number of transforms.

## 7 DISCUSSION

In this work, we measure the intrinsic dimension of popular image datasets and show that the intrinsic dimension of data matters for deep learning. While there may be many factors, such as class separation and the number of classes, which determine generalization, we build the case that intrinsic dimension is one of these important factors. Along the way, we introduce a technique for using GANs to synthesize data while manipulating dimensionality. This technique is useful not only for validating dimension estimation methods but also for examining the learning behavior of neural networks under a dimension-controlled environment. In addition to synthetic data, we verify that dimension plays a large role in learning on natural data. Our results support the commonly held belief that low dimensional structure underpins the success of deep learning on high-resolution data.

These findings raise a number of salient directions for future work. Methods for enhancing neural network learning on high-dimensional data could improve generalization on hard vision problems. To this end, a deeper understanding of the mechanisms by which dimensionality plays a role in learning may enable such methods. Additionally, our work indicates that different dimensionality estimation tools are better suited for different settings. Future work on computing tighter and more reliable estimates specific to image data would allow the community to more precisely study the relationship between the dimensionality of image datasets and learning.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents an empirically grounded study of the intrinsic dimension (ID) of image datasets and its relationship to deep learning generalization. The methodology of using GANs to create ground-truth ID benchmarks is a notable strength. However, the paper suffers from moderate technical concerns regarding the precision of its ID estimation formulas and inconsistent referencing between the text and tables, which could hinder exact reproducibility or interpretation of results.

comments.title: Incomplete or Incorrect MLE Formula
comments.quote: \hat{m}_k(x) = D \text{ (2005)where } T_j(x) \text{ is the }
comments.explanation: The OCR/text in Section 3 contains a significant corruption of the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) formula. While referencing Levina & Bickel (2005), the actual expression provided in the text is missing the core mathematical structure (the summation of log-distances). The text jump from 'Equation (2)' to the MacKay & Ghahramani correction without explicitly displaying the standard MLE formula makes it difficult for a reader to verify if the authors used the standard $k$ or the $(k-1)$ correction mentioned in the text. Given that Equation (2) is cited as the primary estimator, its absence or corruption is a significant technical gap.

comments.title: Cross-reference Inconsistency between Text and Tables
comments.quote: Note that these subsets differ from those used in Table 5, where the estimates are taken from the entire dataset and across all classes.
comments.explanation: The text in Section 6.2 refers to 'Table 5' for the estimates of the full dataset. However, the paper only contains Tables 1, 2, 3, and 4. Based on the context, the authors likely intended to refer to Table 1. This creates confusion for a reader trying to reconcile the class-specific sample complexity results with the global metrics.

comments.title: Insufficient Justification for Latent Dimension as ID Upper Bound
comments.quote: we can a priori upper-bound the intrinsic dimension of generated data by the dimension of the latent noise vector.
comments.explanation: The authors assume the ID of GAN-generated images is bounded by the number of free latent variables ($d_{free}$). While this is a standard intuition, it assumes the generator function $G$ is at least Lipschitz and that the mapping from latent space to image space does not increase dimension. While likely true for standard GANs, the paper lacks a brief justification or reference to the conditions under which this manifold property holds (e.g., the rank of the Jacobian of the generator).

comments.title: Ambiguity in Noise Addition Methodology
comments.quote: where we replace pixels at random in the image.
comments.explanation: In Section 6.3, the authors describe adding $d$-dimensional noise by replacing pixels at random. It is unclear if 'replacing pixels' means completely overriding the pixel value with noise or adding noise to the existing value. Furthermore, if $d$ exceeds the number of pixels ($32 	imes 32 	imes 3 = 3072$), the mapping of a $d$-dimensional hypercube becomes ambiguous. If $d=2560$ pixels are replaced, only 512 original pixels remain, which changes the data distribution significantly beyond just 'increasing dimension'.

comments.title: Questionable Claim regarding MS-COCO Dimensionality
comments.quote: ImageNet... only has intrinsic dimension between 26 and 43; see Figure 1.
comments.explanation: Table 1 and Figure 1 show that MS-COCO has an ID estimate (e.g., 22-37) lower than ImageNet (26-43). Given that MS-COCO images typically contain multiple objects and complex scenes compared to the more centric object-focus of ImageNet, a reader might expect COCO to have a higher ID. The paper does not discuss why the more complex dataset (COCO) yields a lower ID estimate than ImageNet, which might suggest an artifact of the sampling density or a need for more nuanced interpretation of what these tools measure.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-structured investigation into the intrinsic dimension (ID) of image datasets and its impact on deep learning generalization. The authors effectively combine dimension estimation techniques with synthetic data generated via GANs to validate their methodology, which strengthens the credibility of their findings. The experimental design is thoughtful, using multiple datasets and controlled manipulations (e.g., noise injection, data augmentations on FONTS) to isolate the effect of intrinsic dimension. The core claim—that ID strongly influences sample complexity while extrinsic dimension does not—is supported by consistent empirical trends across diverse settings. However, there are several technical and interpretive issues in the presentation of the MLE estimator and its usage, particularly around a garbled equation and potential estimation biases, which, while not invalidating the main conclusions, warrant clarification.

comments.title: Garbled MLE equation obscures estimator definition
comments.quote: (2005)where _T_ propose _j_ ( _x_ ) is theto averageEuclideanthe( _ℓ_2) distancefrom _x_ to its _j[th]_ nearestneighbor.Levina& Bickel¯ _k_ = _n_ 1  _ni_ =1 _[m]_[ˆ] _[k]_[(] _[x][i]_[)][.][MacKay][&][Ghahramani][(2005)][suggestion][a][correction][based][on][averaging][of] inverses
comments.explanation: The text attempts to introduce the MLE estimator from Levina & Bickel (2005), but the equation is severely garbled due to OCR or formatting failure. It appears to mix prose, citation, and formula fragments. The correct MLE estimator for intrinsic dimension at a point is typically given by \(\hat{d}_k(x) = \left( \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^k \log \frac{r_{(k)}(x)}{r_{(j)}(x)} \right)^{-1}\), where \(r_{(j)}(x)\) is the distance to the j-th nearest neighbor. The paper later uses a version averaged over reciprocal estimates—likely \(\bar{m}_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{1}{\hat{d}_k(x_i)}\)—as suggested by MacKay & Ghahramani, but this is only implied. While later results (e.g., convergence to d=10 in Figure 3) suggest the correct estimator is being used in practice, the definition is not mathematically accessible from the text as written. This is not just a formatting issue but prevents a reader from verifying the exact implementation without external reference.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for estimated intrinsic dimension
comments.quote: we expect the intrinsic dimension to increase with _d_[¯] as an upper bound
comments.explanation: The symbol _d_[¯] is introduced without a clear definition. It is later used in Figure 3 as _d_[¯] = 10, presumably denoting the dimension of the free latent subspace in the GAN. However, the overline (bar) notation typically suggests an average or estimator, while here it appears to mean a fixed, controlled parameter. Standard notation would simply use _d_ or _d_latent_. This is not a critical obstruction—the context makes it clear that it refers to the manipulated latent dimension—but the use of a nonstandard symbol with a bar may mislead readers into thinking it is an estimate rather than a ground-truth controlled variable. The same symbol appears in Figure 2 (_d_ = 8, ..., 128), contradicting the bar notation, suggesting an OCR error (e.g., "d̄" vs "d"). Assuming the unbarred d is correct, this is a notation inconsistency due to OCR.

comments.title: Claim of 'no effect' of extrinsic dimension overstates results
comments.quote: Observing the lack of variable growth rates, we see that extrinsic dimension has little to no effect on sample complexity.
comments.explanation: In Section 6.1, the authors state: 'we see that extrinsic dimension has little to no effect on sample complexity' and conclude it is 'irrelevant'. The evidence in Figure 5 shows that test accuracy curves for different resolutions (3x16x16 to 3x128x128) nearly overlap, supporting a weak dependence. However, the curves in Figure 5 (reconstructed from the text) show a small but consistent vertical offset, with higher resolution models achieving slightly higher accuracy. While this effect is much smaller than that of intrinsic dimension, claiming 'no effect' is too strong. In deep learning, even small architectural or resolution changes can have measurable impacts due to inductive biases, filter coverage, or optimization dynamics. A more accurate claim would be that extrinsic dimension has a negligible effect compared to intrinsic dimension. This overstatement slightly weakens the logical precision of the conclusion but does not invalidate the core insight.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for noise-based dimension increase
comments.quote: we add independent noise, drawn uniformly from a fixed randomly oriented _d_-dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, to each sample in a dataset. This procedure ensures that the dataset has dimension at least _d_.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that adding d-dimensional noise ensures the dataset has intrinsic dimension at least d. While intuitively plausible, this is non-trivial and lacks justification. If the original data lies on a low-dimensional manifold and noise is added in a d-dimensional subspace, the resulting support may not necessarily have dimension equal to the sum, due to potential overlaps or nonlinear interactions. A rigorous argument would require assumptions about transversality or independence of the noise subspace and the data manifold. Moreover, if the noise is added only along randomly selected pixels (as stated: 'replace pixels at random'), the effective dimension introduced may be less than d if the pixel space is treated as discrete. The empirical increase in estimated ID (Table 3) supports the qualitative trend, but the theoretical justification for 'at least d' is underspecified.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting investigation into the intrinsic dimension of image datasets and its impact on learning. The authors use MLE to estimate intrinsic dimensions and find that natural image datasets have low intrinsic dimensions, which correlates with sample complexity and generalization. While the experimental results are compelling and the validation approach using GANs is innovative, there are significant issues with the presentation of the MLE formula, which is completely corrupted and unreadable. This makes it difficult to understand or reproduce the main method used in the paper. Additionally, some experimental procedures are not clearly specified, particularly the noise addition method and the anchor fraction selection.

comments.title: Corrupted MLE Formula
comments.quote: (2005)where T propose j ( x ) is theto averageEuclideanthe( ℓ 2) distanceestimatesfromat x eachto itspoint j[th] nearestto obtainneighbor.a globalLevinaestimate& m Bickel¯ k = n 1  ni =1 [m][ˆ] [k][(] [x][i]_[)][.][MacKay][&][Ghahramani][(2005)][suggestion][a][correction][based][on][averaging][of] inverses
comments.explanation: The formula for MLE intrinsic dimension estimation in Section 3 is completely corrupted and unreadable. This is a critical issue as it's the main method used throughout the paper. The formula appears to contain garbled text and mathematical symbols, making it impossible to understand how the MLE estimation is performed. While I attempted to reconstruct what might be intended based on the textual description (that it involves distances to k-nearest neighbors and averaging of inverses), the actual mathematical formulation is completely lost. This significantly hinders the reproducibility of the paper and the reader's ability to understand the core methodology. Even assuming this is an OCR error, the formula as presented is unintelligible, which is a major issue for a paper centered on this estimation technique.

comments.title: Underspecified Noise Addition Method
comments.quote: We thus add independent noise, drawn uniformly from a fixed randomly oriented d-dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space, to each sample in a dataset.
comments.explanation: The method for adding noise in Section 6.3 is not clearly specified. The paper mentions adding noise from a 'fixed randomly oriented d-dimensional unit hypercube embedded in pixel space' but doesn't explain how this embedding is achieved. This is crucial for reproducing the experiments and understanding the nature of the noise being added. The paper doesn't specify how to select the orientation of the hypercube, how to map points from the hypercube to pixel space, or how to ensure the noise has the intended dimensionality. Without these details, it's impossible to replicate this key experiment that demonstrates how adding noise affects intrinsic dimension and generalization.

comments.title: Underspecified Anchor Fraction Selection
comments.quote: In this approach, we randomly select a fraction α of the dataset as anchors. Then, we evaluate the MLE estimate using only the anchor points, where nearest-neighbors are computed over the entire dataset.
comments.explanation: The method for selecting the fraction α of anchors for large datasets is not clearly explained. The paper mentions using this approach to scale to large datasets like ImageNet but doesn't specify how to choose the value of α or how this method affects the accuracy of the estimates. The paper claims this acceleration has 'no impact on the expected value of the result' when anchors are chosen randomly, but doesn't provide any theoretical justification or empirical evaluation of this claim in the main text (only a reference to Appendix A.3). Without knowing how α was chosen or understanding the trade-offs involved, readers cannot determine if this approximation is appropriate for their own datasets.

comments.title: Potential Overstatement of Novelty
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first experimental demonstration that intrinsic but not extrinsic dimensionality matters for the generalization of deep networks.
comments.explanation: The claim about being the first to demonstrate the relationship between intrinsic dimension and generalization might be overstating the novelty. There is prior work (e.g., Narayanan & Niyogi, 2009) that has theoretically established this relationship, and other empirical studies might have touched on this as well. The paper itself cites learning theory work showing that 'the number of samples needed to learn a well-conditioned decision boundary between two classes is exponential in the intrinsic dimension of the manifold on which the classes lie.' While the experimental verification with neural networks may be novel, the claim of being the 'first experimental demonstration' seems too strong without a more comprehensive review of prior empirical work. A more nuanced statement acknowledging prior theoretical work would be more accurate.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling empirical study on the intrinsic dimension (ID) of image datasets and its relationship to deep learning generalization. The methodology is generally sound, particularly the use of GANs and font synthesis to create ground-truth environments for ID control. However, the presentation of the primary mathematical estimator in Section 3 contains an OCR-mangled formula and a subsequent paragraph that is essentially unreadable, which obscures the formal definition of the estimator used throughout the rest of the paper. While the context allows one to deduce that the authors are using the Levina & Bickel MLE estimator, the lack of a clear, coherent formula makes technical verification of their stated methodology difficult.

comments.title: Unreadable MLE formula and surrounding text
comments.quote: (2005)where _T_ propose _j_ ( _x_ ) is theto averageEuclideanthe( _ℓ_ local2) distanceestimatesfromat _x_ eachto itspoint _j[th]_ nearestto obtainneighbor.a globalLevinaestimate& _m_ Bickel¯ _k_ = _n_ 1  _ni_ =1 _[m]_[ˆ] _[k]_[(] _[x][i]_[)][.][MacKay][&][Ghahramani][(2005)][suggestion][a][correction][based][on][averaging][of] inverses
comments.explanation: The text describing the MLE estimator is severely corrupted by OCR errors. The formula for the global estimate is mangled: it appears to attempt to write a summation over nearest-neighbor distances, but the symbols and formatting are broken beyond recognition. While the reference to Levina & Bickel (2005) provides a path to reconstruct the intended math, the manuscript itself fails to provide a readable definition of the core method used in all subsequent experiments.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'noise dimensionality'
comments.quote: We use the following noise dimensionalities: 256 , 512 , 1024 , 2048 , 2560.
comments.explanation: In Section 6.3, the authors describe adding noise from a 'd-dimensional unit hypercube' to images in pixel space. It is unclear if this 'd' refers to the subspace dimensionality of the added noise or the total intrinsic dimension of the augmented dataset. Given that 2560 is significantly lower than the ambient dimension of a 32x32x3 image (3072 pixels), it is ambiguous how this noise is projected or embedded. While the trends in the figures remain interpretable, the exact procedure for generating these specific noise patterns is too underspecified to reproduce.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper provides a compelling empirical investigation into the intrinsic dimension (ID) of natural image datasets and its relationship to generalization in deep learning. The authors validate MLE-based ID estimation using GAN-generated data with controllable dimensionality, measure the ID of several popular datasets (finding surprisingly low values), and conduct controlled experiments showing that intrinsic—but not extrinsic—dimensionality impacts sample complexity. The work is timely and well-motivated, and the conclusions are broadly supported by the evidence. However, the paper contains a few clear errors and inconsistencies. Most notably, the experimental description in Section 6.1 is internally contradictory regarding the number and sizes of synthetic datasets used to test extrinsic dimensionality, which undermines reproducibility. Additionally, references to a non-existent 'Table 5' appear multiple times. These issues, while localized, should be corrected to improve clarity and trustworthiness.

comments.title: Incorrect reference to non-existent table
comments.quote: Using three different parameter settings for the MLE ID estimator, we find that the ID is indeed much smaller than the number of pixels; see Table 5.
comments.explanation: The paper refers to 'Table 5' twice (once in Section 5 and again in Section 6.2). However, the paper only contains Tables 1–4. This is clearly a LaTeX cross-referencing error. The intended table is almost certainly Table 1, which lists MLE estimates for popular datasets. While a reader can infer the correct table from context, the error is confusing and suggests sloppy proofreading. It should be corrected to maintain professionalism and avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of synthetic dataset sizes for extrinsic dimension experiment
comments.quote: We create four synthetic datasets of varying extrinsic dimensionality by resizing the images with nearest-neighbor interpolation. Specifically, we create 6 datasets of square, 3-channel images of sizes 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, fixed intrinsic dimensionality of size 128, and all other experimental details the same.
comments.explanation: The text contains multiple numeric inconsistencies: it first says 'four synthetic datasets,' then '6 datasets,' but lists only five sizes (16, 32, 64, 128, 256). Moreover, Figure 5 shows only four curves corresponding to sizes 16, 32, 64, and 128; the size 256 is not plotted. This makes it impossible to determine exactly how many and which resolutions were tested. The claim that extrinsic dimension has little effect may still hold, but the experimental setup is underspecified and contradictory. This is a moderate issue because it affects reproducibility and the clarity of a key finding.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a valuable empirical investigation into the intrinsic dimensionality (ID) of image datasets and its correlation with neural network generalization. The authors successfully leverage GANs to create synthetic datasets of controlled ID, providing a robust validation framework. The empirical evidence strongly supports the intuition that low intrinsic dimensionality facilitates learning. However, the reviewer identified a potential artifact in the dimension estimation method regarding image resolution, noting a drastic drop in estimated ID when resizing high-resolution images to a lower resolution (Table 1 vs. Table 2). Furthermore, the definition of the training termination criterion in the sample complexity experiments is slightly underspecified, which limits the reproducibility of the exact sample complexity numbers.

comments.title: Discrepancy in ImageNet intrinsic dimension estimates across resolutions
comments.quote:
For example, we estimate that ImageNet, despite containing 224 _×_ 224 _×_ 3 = 150528 pixels per image, only has intrinsic dimension between 26 and 43; see Figure 1. 

... Because these datasets are ordinarily of varying extrinsic dimensionality, we resize all to size 32 _×_ 32 _×_ 3 (before applying MLE). We report results in Figure 6...
comments.explanation: The authors report the intrinsic dimension of ImageNet as approximately 26-43 when estimated on the native 224x224 resolution (Table 1). Later, when the same dataset is resized to 32x32 for comparison (Table 2), the estimated dimension drops to approximately 15-22. Intrinsic dimensionality is theoretically an invariant property of the underlying manifold representing the data class. A drastic drop of ~50% due to downsampling is highly suspicious and suggests that the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) method employed is extremely sensitive to the density of pixel representation. If the estimator effectively measures the 'pixel grid dimensionality' rather than the 'data manifold dimensionality,' it undermines the claim that ID is an intrinsic property of the images and introduces a significant source of variability when comparing datasets of different resolutions.

comments.title: Underspecified training termination criterion
comments.quote: We then train models until they fit their entire training set with increasing amounts of training samples and measure the test error.
comments.explanation: The text states that models are trained 'until they fit their entire training set,' but does not define what constitutes 'fitting.' In the context of neural networks, this could imply reaching zero training loss (overfitting), reaching a specific training accuracy threshold (e.g., 99%), or simply converging to a local minimum. Figure 4 (Synthetic GAN Data) shows test accuracy curves converging to near 100%. However, the 'sample complexity' metric (number of training samples needed to achieve a given test error) is sensitive to how training terminates. If 'fitting' implies forcing the training loss to zero via continued training (which effectively increases samples available to the model in a sequential setting or implies the model has capacity), this conflates sample complexity with model capacity.

comments.title: Typographical error in table header
comments.quote: Table 4: Mean and standard errror of MLE estimates...
comments.explanation: The header of Table 4 contains a typo: 'standard errror' is spelled with a repeated 'r'. This is a minor formatting issue that does not affect the substance of the result but is noticeable in the presentation.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a clear and well-motivated empirical investigation into the intrinsic dimension (ID) of image datasets and its relationship to sample complexity and generalization. The experimental design leveraging GANs, noise injection, and data augmentation is thoughtful, and the core findings align with established learning theory. However, there are a few technical inaccuracies and ambiguities that should be addressed. Most notably, the paper misstates the bias-variance tradeoff for the MLE estimator it relies upon, contains a heavily corrupted equation due to PDF extraction, and uses imprecise language regarding training stopping criteria. These issues are mostly localized and do not fundamentally undermine the central conclusions, but correcting them would strengthen methodological rigor, clarify reproducibility, and prevent misleading takeaways for readers applying these dimension estimation tools.
